does: This paper introduces MLLMGuard, a comprehensive safety evaluation suite designed to systematically assess multimodal large language models across multiple risk dimensions. The suite comprises three main components: a bilingual image-text evaluation dataset covering English and Chinese, inference utilities for testing, and a lightweight evaluator module. The evaluation framework addresses five critical safety dimensions—Privacy, Bias, Toxicity, Truthfulness, and Legality—each decomposed into specific subtasks. A key methodological contribution is the development of an independent evaluator that avoids the circularity and bias inherent in using GPT-4V both as evaluator and evaluated model. The suite provides both quantitative metrics and qualitative analysis across these dimensions, offering researchers a more rigorous and robust alternative to existing safety benchmarks that typically lack comprehensive coverage or exhibit methodological limitations.
connects: This work builds on the emerging focus on safety evaluation for large language models, extending earlier safety assessment frameworks developed for text-only LLMs to the multimodal setting. It responds to known limitations in existing MLLM benchmarks that either incorporate safety considerations superficially or rely on problematic evaluation methodologies. The paper engages with the broader research trajectory on AI safety, fairness, and alignment, particularly efforts to systematize evaluation across multiple risk dimensions simultaneously. It draws on established safety taxonomies from NLP and computer vision literature—including concepts of privacy violations, demographic bias, toxic content, hallucination detection, and legal compliance—while adapting these frameworks to the specific challenges posed by vision-language models. The bilingual design connects to growing efforts in evaluating non-English models and addressing geographic representation gaps in AI safety research.
enables: MLLMGuard establishes infrastructure for more systematic red-teaming and safety improvement of multimodal models, enabling researchers to identify specific failure modes across different risk categories rather than treating safety monolithically. The lightweight independent evaluator design opens pathways for developing better automated safety assessment tools that can scale beyond manual annotation. The bilingual evaluation dataset supports comparative analysis of safety behaviors across languages, potentially revealing systemic differences in model behavior by linguistic community. This work enables future research into interpretability and mitigation strategies by providing fine-grained categorization of safety failures. The suite can serve as a benchmark for evaluating alignment techniques, filtering methods, and constitutional AI approaches tailored to multimodal models. It also facilitates stakeholder engagement with safety concerns, as the multidimensional framework provides concrete, auditable evidence of model risks relevant to deployment decisions and regulatory oversight.
